column9: Shelter is the core human consumption function, and it absorbs the largest recurring share of household budgets.
Medical spending is supported by demographics, chronic care demand, and high labor and facility intensity.
Communications, education, professional help, and social services are diffuse but collectively very large.
Fast-turn, everyday-use items create large aggregate spend despite modest ticket sizes.
Insurance spending rises with underlying asset values, claims costs, and medical utilization.

column3: Food and beverages purchased for off-premises consumption
column9: Groceries are universal, repetitive spending, so even low single-digit growth compounds into very large dollar totals.
Eating away from home layers wages, rent, and service margins onto basic calorie demand.
Households continue to spend on leisure, media, and entertainment when income and confidence hold up.
Transportation needs keep autos and parts near the top of the durable-goods stack.
Transport demand follows commuting, travel, freight-linked ticketing, and maintenance of personal mobility.
Entertainment hardware and sports gear remain meaningful discretionary goods categories.

column3: Final consumption expenditures of nonprofit institutions serving households (NPISHs)
column9: Nonprofit-provided household services are economically real consumption even when paid indirectly.
Apparel is a steady refresh category shaped by seasons, fashion cycles, and value-channel pricing.

Fuel spending moves with miles driven and commodity prices, making it one of the more volatile household goods lines.
Small but durable household and personal items add up because of broad usage.
